sites.name: au-unis
sites.description: Every Australian university joined into one interface from seven separate Department of Education workbooks — 42 institutions, 1,676,077 students, ten years of series, covering enrolments, international share, attrition, retention, subject success, completions, student-staff ratios and field mix. Two findings carry the site. First, the ATAR gradient: 93.7% of school leavers admitted with an ATAR of 95-100 complete a bachelor degree within nine years against 55.0% of those admitted at 30-49, a 38.7-point gap. Second, and the reason the signature view exists: the four-year completion rate everyone quotes (41.5%) is NOT the failure rate, because 35.5% of that cohort are still enrolled — plenty of degrees run longer than four years and part-time students longer again. Given nine years, 70.5% have completed. The 'Who finishes' view therefore defaults to nine years and teaches the caveat through its 4/6/9-year toggle: moving it visibly drains the grey 'still enrolled' band into the teal 'completed' band, so the user learns the trap by operating the control rather than reading a footnote. The other headline: 598,882 of 1,676,077 students (35.7%) have a permanent home residence outside Australia, a larger group than NSW and Victoria combined and the single largest origin in the country; Murdoch is 57.4% international, Sydney 50.8%, RMIT 50.0%. Eight views: rankings across 8 measures with the national median marked, the cohort-outcome decomposition sliced by ATAR/SES/disability/First Nations/regionality and 10 more attributes, a log-scaled exposure scatter quadranted by medians with zoom/pan and label de-collision that avoids both other labels and the dots themselves, the 9x10 origin-destination matrix as Sankey + choropleth + exact numbers, a squarified treemap plus institution x field heatmap, ten years annotated with the 2020 border closure and 2024 visa tightening, a sortable Explorer with sparklines, and auto-detected insights — plus a hash-linkable per-university drill-down (#i=3035). Built around EIGHT traps that each produce a confidently wrong number: (1) aggregate rows (National Total, Table A Providers, State Total, and a per-state Non-University Higher Education Institutions bucket) sit in the same column as real universities, so ranking them together counts the country several times; (2) np / '< 5' / '.' are string sentinels in numeric columns where Number(x)||0 gives a confident zero — a withheld cell is 1-4, never 0; (3) zeros in the historic rate series mean 'not a reporting provider yet' — Avondale reads 0 attrition for 2014-2022 then 12.65, and left alone ranks as the best-performing university in Australia; (4) sector attrition (domestic, transfers don't count as leaving) and provider attrition (overseas, transfers do) have different denominators and never share an axis; (5) the four-year completion trap above; (6) footnote markers and provider codes are welded into institution names inconsistently between workbooks, so the join key must be normalised or the same university appears twice; (7) the staff workbooks span headers over three merged rows with two metric blocks side by side; (8) footnote paragraphs are written into data columns in Section 17 and the time series. A ninth lives in the frontend: the interstate map excludes overseas students on purpose — include them and every state is an importer, so the map shades one uniform colour and the interstate story (Tasmania takes in domestic students at 1.47x, Queensland loses them at 0.87x) disappears entirely. The pipeline is dependency-free — its own minimal zip + xlsx reader, so CI never installs anything — and asserts its parsed national total reproduces the department's published 1,676,077 exactly, failing the build on drift. education.gov.au refuses bare fetch() clients and initially failed on CI with connect timeouts; fixed with full browser headers (incl. Sec-Fetch-*) plus dns ipv4first, since GitHub runners advertise IPv6 and the host only half-answers on it.
data_sources: Dept of Education Selected Higher Education Statistics — Student Data Sections 2, 7, 14, 15, 17 (education.gov.au)
Dept of Education Student Summary Time Series (education.gov.au)
Dept of Education Staff Data Appendix 2 — Student-Staff Ratios (education.gov.au)

sites.name: au-insolvency
sites.description: Every personal insolvency in Australia — bankruptcies, debt agreements and personal insolvency agreements — for all 340 SA3 regions across 75 quarters (Sep 2007 to Mar 2026), joined to ABS resident population so regions are comparable and to real ABS ASGS 2021 boundaries so they are mappable. Headline finding is counter-intuitive: formal insolvency has COLLAPSED, 3,161 in the latest quarter against a peak of 9,863 in Sep 2009 (68% lower) during a cost-of-living crisis — driven not by households becoming solvent but by the June 2019 debt agreement reforms (debt agreements down 65%, never recovered) and pandemic-era support and forbearance. The dataset measures entry into a formal process, so when that process becomes harder to enter, distress leaves the statistics rather than the economy. Geographically concentrated in outer-suburban growth corridors: Caboolture tops the country at 16.0 per 10,000 adults (3.2x the national median of 5.0), with Beenleigh, Browns Plains and Jimboomba close behind; 26 of 331 ranked regions carry a quarter of the national total. Nine views: rankings across 3 metrics, SA3 choropleth with 4 measures and a skew-aware quantile ramp, the signature Trajectory scatter (current rate against four-year change, quadranted by the national medians with zoom/pan — the only form that separates a region that has always struggled from one deteriorating fast, which a league table cannot), searchable Explorer with 75-quarter sparklines, annotated 19-year timeline, administration-type mix plus creditor-forced vs voluntary bankruptcy and a state x type matrix, business-vs-consumer told at the level where it is reliable, a click-through distribution histogram, and auto-detected insights — plus hash-linkable per-region drill-downs (#v=map&r=31502). Built around FOUR traps that each produce confidently wrong numbers: (1) 16 GCCSA aggregate rows sit in the same ASGS Code column as the SA3s they contain, so ranking them together counts the country twice; (2) the type column carries two levels of subtotal (Total bankruptcies = debtor's petitions + sequestration orders; Total personal insolvencies = everything), and summing naively double-counts every bankruptcy — the pipeline asserts its leaf sum reproduces AFSA's own published total exactly (3,161 for Mar 2026) and fails the build on drift; (3) 'Data not available' is a string sentinel in a numeric column 18,908 times, where Number() gives NaN but parseInt/||0 give a convincing 0; (4) suppression is COMPLEMENTARY — both sides of the business/consumer split are withheld together so neither is recoverable by subtraction (8,259 paired vs 50 single), and region totals are withheld too (8.9% of cells). Because genuine zeros ARE published as 0, a withheld quarter is 1-2 cases, so affected 12-month totals are midpoint estimates with an explicit range; treating them as zero had reported populous Sydney regions (Hawkesbury, Manly) as having no insolvencies at all, and fixing it also changed the 'deteriorating fastest' finding from a noise artifact to Melton - Bacchus Marsh. A fifth quirk: ABS age code A59 means ages 5-9, not 55-59, and sorts between A55 and A60 — summing adult bands by name puts children in the per-capita denominator. The business/consumer split is only ~72% published nationally and usable in 112 of 340 regions, so it is reported at state level with the withheld share disclosed rather than made the headline SA3 metric.
data_sources: AFSA regional quarterly personal insolvency time series (afsa.gov.au)
AFSA quarterly personal insolvencies by administration type and state (afsa.gov.au)
ABS Estimated Resident Population by SA3 and age, ERP_ASGS2021 (data.api.abs.gov.au)
sites.notes: Deployed and verified over http:// (live bundle hash matches local dist byte-for-byte; all data and SEO assets 200). Let's Encrypt cert was still issuing at hand-over after a ~15-minute poll and two CNAME cycles — normal for a freshly pointed domain. Re-check https_enforced and flip status to deployed.

sites.name: au-trade
sites.description: Everything Australia sells the world and buys back — the ABS merchandise trade collection (224 partners, 342 SITC commodity codes, 359 months from July 1996) turned into nine views. Over the 12 months to May 2026: $530.2bn exported, $490.0bn imported, a $40.2bn goods surplus, with China taking 34.8% of exports — more than Japan, South Korea, the US, Hong Kong and India combined — and four commodity groups (ores, gold, coal, gas) making up 66% of everything sold. Built around the dataset's defining trap: ABS confidentialises destinations far more heavily at commodity level than at the national total, so while only 1.6% of exports nationally lack a country, 96.3% of the $57.4bn natural gas export has no published buyer. Every concentration figure is therefore computed over published destinations only and labelled with the withheld share; commodities whose destinations are mostly hidden are drawn hollow in the signature Exposure scatter (value against share-to-largest-buyer, the quadrant where magnitude meets concentration) instead of being plotted as though their position were known; and a dedicated Blind Spots view ranks the $71.2bn of withheld destinations and explains why SITC 98 'combined confidential items' ($4.44bn) is not a product. Nine views: partner leaderboard with 30-year sparklines, log-scaled Natural Earth world choropleth plus an AU state-of-origin mode (quantiles were rejected — they shade China and Canada identically and hide the concentration the map exists to show), the Exposure scatter with zoom/pan and greedy label de-collision, a section→partner Sankey with hover-to-isolate, a searchable commodity explorer with squarified treemap, a section×partner matrix separating broad buyers from single-commodity ones, 30 years of monthly trade annotated with the China boom/GFC/LNG ramp/COVID/2020 China restrictions, Blind Spots, and auto-detected insights — plus hash-linkable drill-downs per partner (#c=CHIN) and commodity (#s=28). Data notes: country aggregates (OECD, ASEAN) and pseudo-destinations (No Country Details, ship stores) are excluded from every ranking; the import series starts July 2000 against exports' July 1996, so it is nulled rather than zero-filled; commodity detail does not sum to country totals because suppression is level-dependent.
data_sources: ABS Merchandise Exports by Commodity (SITC), Country and State — MERCH_EXP (data.api.abs.gov.au)
ABS Merchandise Imports by Commodity (SITC), Country and State — MERCH_IMP (data.api.abs.gov.au)
ABS SDMX codelists CL_MERCH_SITC / CL_MERCH_COUNTRY / CL_MERCH_STATE

sites.name: au-welfare
sites.description: Every Australian government payment by recipient count and per-capita rate, for all 2,454 SA2 areas (roughly suburbs) and all 150 federal electorates — the DSS quarterly payment demographics joined to ABS Estimated Resident Population by age, which is what turns unreadable counts into comparable rates. Built around the fact that the Age Pension is 2.6m of the country's 5.4m income support recipients, so any welfare measure that includes it mostly measures how old an area is: Tea Gardens - Hawks Nest posts a 40.0% headline rate but only 24.9% of its working-age population is on a payment (a 15.2-point gap that is age structure, not hardship), while Elizabeth in Adelaide runs the other way at 50.2% headline against 61.5% working-age. The site's default measure is therefore the working-age rate (income support excluding the Age Pension over the 15-64 population, 15.7% nationally), and its signature view is a scatter of headline against working-age rate with an agreement diagonal — the only form that can separate a retirement town from a distressed one. A third derived metric, Age Pension take-up (recipients over the 65+ population), reads as a wealth measure because the pension is means-tested: 55.9% nationally but 12.2% in Bellevue Hill and 13.3% in Toorak. Nine views: SA2 choropleth across 6 measures, the Pension Illusion scatter with zoom/pan and ranked side panels, rankings against the national median, searchable Explorer with 12-quarter sparklines, a counts-only electorate map and payment-family composition, a region x payment-family mix heatmap (Bribie Island 70% pension vs Dandenong North 31% unemployment), 173 months of national history with the April 2020 JobSeeker doubling annotated, a distribution histogram that click-throughs into a filtered Explorer, and auto-detected insights — plus a hash-linkable per-suburb drill-down. Data notes: DSS rounds every count to the nearest 5 so rankings apply a 5,000-resident floor; supplements and concession cards are never summed into the income support total because people hold them alongside a payment; rates are suppressed (not clamped) where the denominator is too small, after industrial-estate SA2s produced 500% take-up rates against a 65+ population of 1; electorates carry no per-capita rates because the ABS publishes no population for the 2024 electoral boundaries.
data_sources: DSS Payment Demographics by 2024 Commonwealth Electoral Division (data.gov.au)
DSS Income Support Recipients Monthly Time Series, Jan 2012 onwards (data.gov.au)
ABS Estimated Resident Population by SA2 and age, ERP_ASGS2021 (data.api.abs.gov.au)
Digital Atlas of Australia — Federal Electoral Divisions March 2025

sites.name: au-medicines
sites.description: Every prescription medicine subsidised by the Australian Government, as actually dispensed — the PBS/RPBS date-of-supply reports (2,047,223 rows, 12,119 item codes, 69 months from Jul 2020 to Mar 2026) joined to the item–drug map and the WHO ATC classification, giving 1.88bn prescriptions of 1,013 medicines costing $68.27bn in Commonwealth subsidy and $18.28bn out of patients' pockets. Built around the fact that volume and cost are two different stories: only 5 of the 20 most-prescribed medicines are also in the 20 most expensive, and 31% of all prescriptions (582m) are dispensed under the co-payment so the government pays nothing at all. The costliest medicine on the scheme is aflibercept, an eye injection, at $2.69bn — ahead of every heart, cancer or diabetes drug; menopause hormone therapy is up 43.5x year on year after its 2025 listing; patients self-fund 85% of the combined oral contraceptive; one lanadelumab script costs $29,290. Eight views: rankings across five metrics, a searchable Explorer of all 1,013 medicines with 69-month sparklines, a click-to-zoom radial sunburst of the full ATC tree (body system → drug class → medicine), a log-log cost-vs-volume scatter with equal-spend diagonals plus zoom/pan, slope charts of the fastest risers and fallers with algorithmic label de-collision, 69 months stacked by body system annotated with the co-payment cuts and 60-day dispensing, a who-pays view with a body-system × patient-category heatmap, and auto-detected insights — plus a hash-linkable per-medicine drill-down with rank, monthly series, cost-per-script trend, patient/pharmacy mix and constituent PBS items. Data note: the department's PATIENT_CONTRIB column fills under-co-payment scripts with the maximum schedule price rather than what was paid (overstating patient spend ~$300m/yr), so PATIENT_NET_CONTRIB is used throughout. No map — the dataset has no geography.
data_sources: PBS and RPBS Section 85/100 Date of Supply supplementary reports, 6 financial-year CSVs (pbs.gov.au)
PBS item drug map — item code to generic name and ATC5 (pbs.gov.au)

sites.name: au-income
sites.description: What Australians actually earn in every postcode — the ATO's 2023-24 taxation statistics (16,379,323 individuals across 2,283 postcodes, Tables 6/7/8) joined to real ABS ASGS 2021 postal-area boundaries. Built around the median-vs-average gap, the measure that separates two kinds of 'rich postcode' an income ranking makes look identical: Toorak's median is $82,873 but its average is $277,708 (3.35x — a few enormous incomes), while Pilbara mining towns hold the highest medians in the country (Fortescue $142,580, Rocklea $111,679) with averages level with or below them (1.03x — everyone earns well, no rich tail). The typical Toorak earner makes less than a Karratha mine worker; with a 1,000-taxpayer floor the top 8 medians nationally are all mining postcodes and Double Bay is 11th. Eight views: Leaflet choropleth of 2,266 postal areas across 7 metrics, the signature log-log Gap scatter with the average=median diagonal, rankings, searchable Explorer with 20-year sparklines, postcode x ANZSCO occupation heatmap (Blackwater 27% Machinery Operators vs Erskineville 38% Professionals), a click-through distribution histogram plus national income-band composition, 20 years of median income by state on a real time axis, and auto-detected insights (The Ponds negatively gears at 25.7%, 4x the typical postcode; 30% of Brunswick still owes HELP debt) — plus a hash-linkable per-postcode drill-down with rank, 20-year history, income/age/occupation mix, deductions, negative gearing and HELP debt.
data_sources: ATO Taxation Statistics 2023-24 — Individuals Table 6 (161 items by postcode) (data.gov.au)
ATO Taxation Statistics 2023-24 — Individuals Table 7 (income/age/occupation bands by postcode) (data.gov.au)
ATO Taxation Statistics 2023-24 — Individuals Table 8 (median + average income, 12 year-points) (data.gov.au)
ABS ASGS 2021 Postal Area digital boundaries (abs.gov.au)

sites.name: au-solar
sites.description: Every rooftop solar system and home battery in Australia by postcode — 4,437,265 solar systems (Apr 2001–May 2026) and 401,185 batteries since the Cheaper Home Batteries rebate began in July 2025, from the Clean Energy Regulator's SRES postcode data, joined to ABS Census 2021 dwelling counts so the numbers are per-home rather than raw counts. Signature finding: owner-occupied detached-house share explains ~40% of the gap between postcodes (r=0.63, n=2,079) — Angle Vale SA has 203 systems per 100 homes while South Wharf in Melbourne (98% apartments) has 0.4, a 508× gap driven by the renter/apartment split incentive. Eight views: Leaflet choropleth of all 2,640 ABS postal areas with 4 metrics, the Solar Divide scatter with fitted trend line and R², rankings leaderboard, searchable Explorer with sparklines, the battery rebate surge month by month, a 25-year state-stacked timeline annotated with policy milestones, a distribution histogram with click-through, and auto-detected insights — plus a hash-linkable per-postcode drill-down with rank, tenure/dwelling mix and monthly history.
data_sources: Clean Energy Regulator — small-scale installation postcode data (cer.gov.au)
ABS Census 2021 General Community Profile, Postal Areas, table G37 (abs.gov.au)
ABS ASGS 2021 Postal Area digital boundaries (abs.gov.au)

sites.name: au-mp-expenses
sites.description: Every taxpayer-funded work expense claimed by Australia's federal MPs and Senators since the 2022 election — the full IPEA quarterly expenditure data (15 quarters, Jul 2022–Mar 2026, ~$618M, 334 current & former parliamentarians) unified into one interface. Eight views: a party-coloured Rankings leaderboard (rank by total or any category), a searchable Explorer table with quarterly sparklines, a Leaflet choropleth of all 150 federal electorates shaded by member spend, a category treemap + party×category per-member heatmap, party total-vs-average comparison, a quarterly trend chart with the 2025 election marked, a per-parliamentarian distribution histogram, and auto-detected Insights, plus a hash-linkable per-person drill-down with category breakdown, quarterly trend and biggest individual claims. Headline: PM Anthony Albanese tops at ~$15.3M; Office Administration and Office Facilities each account for ~28% of all spending.
data_sources: IPEA Current and Former Parliamentarians' Expenditure (data.gov.au)
Digital Atlas of Australia — Federal Electoral Divisions March 2025 (digital.atlas.gov.au)

sites.name: au-ndis-watch
sites.description: Search every NDIS Commission compliance action — banning orders, registration revocations, suspensions, and notices against Australian disability providers and workers — by name, state, and severity.

sites.name: au-visas
sites.description: How many people live in Australia on each temporary visa — 2.9 million holders by nationality, visa type and over 14 years, from the official Home Affairs data, mapped, ranked and charted.

sites.name: pipboy
sites.description: An unofficial Pip-Boy 3000 homage — S.P.E.C.I.A.L. stats, six holotape mini-games, a hand-drawn wasteland map, six radio stations, and CRT phosphor glow.

sites.name: voynich-investigation
sites.description: A forensic, statistics-first reading of the Voynich Manuscript — an interactive report styled after a botanical dichotomous key.

sites.name: benrichardson.dev
sites.description: A pixel-perfect 1984 Macintosh System 1 desktop in the browser — draggable windows, nine desk accessories, fourteen games, and a pile of easter eggs.

sites.name: au-immunisation
sites.description: Childhood immunisation coverage for every Australian SA3 region, measured against the 95% herd-immunity line — unifying the Australian Immunisation Register's per-state (SA3) and per-PHN (all children + First Nations) releases with ABS ASGS 2021 boundaries. Eight views: SA3 choropleth map with per-region drill-down, priority leaderboard, searchable Explorer of every region × vaccine, vaccine × state heatmap, First Nations equity (all vs Aboriginal & Torres Strait Islander per PHN, ranked by gap), 2015–2025 national trend, coverage-distribution histogram, and auto-detected insights. Headline: most regions now sit below 95% and coverage has slipped since its 2020 peak.
data_sources: Australian Immunisation Register — childhood immunisation coverage (PHN + SA3), Dept of Health, Disability and Ageing (health.gov.au)

sites.name: au-super
sites.description: Compare every Australian MySuper default super product — all 52 products (37 trustees, $1.21tn member assets, as at 30 June 2025) from APRA's Comprehensive Product Performance Package. 3/5/7/10-year net investment returns, total fees at five balance sizes, the annual performance-test result and margin over benchmark, growth allocation and member numbers. Seven views: sortable Explorer, Rankings with metric toggle, a Fees-vs-Returns scatter with the low-fee/high-return sweet spot shaded, a fee heatmap across balance tiers, a trustee treemap, a returns/fees distribution histogram, and auto-detected Insights, plus a hash-linkable per-product drill-down with the lifecycle glide-path.
data_sources: APRA MySuper Comprehensive Product Performance Package (apra.gov.au)

sites.name: au-relations
sites.description: How Australia formally relates to every other government — the full DFAT Australian Treaties Database (4,512 treaty records since 1901), the complete overseas diplomatic network including accreditation coverage, all 19 FTAs and curated organisation memberships, joined per country. Seven views: countries leaderboard with sparklines, 3-mode Leaflet world map, searchable treaty database, 125-year timeline, force-directed embassy accreditation network, subject × country matrix, and auto-insights, plus a hash-linkable per-country drill-down.
data_sources: DFAT Australian Treaties Database (docs.dfat.gov.au JSON API)
DFAT embassies and consulates overseas list (dfat.gov.au)

sites.name: au-jobs
sites.description: Interactive explorer for the Small Area Labour Markets dataset — smoothed unemployment rate, unemployed persons and labour force for all ~544 Australian local government areas across 62 quarters (Dec 2010–Mar 2026). Seven views: Leaflet choropleth, leaderboard, sortable table with sparklines, 15-year state trend lines, state comparison + quarter heatmap, distribution histogram, and auto-detected insights, plus a hash-linkable per-LGA drill-down.
data_sources: Small Area Labour Markets (Smoothed LGA), Jobs and Skills Australia / DEWR
ABS ASGS 2024 Local Government Area boundaries

sites.name: au-pay-gap

Seven views: explorer, industry rankings, glass-pyramid representation, distribution histogram, industry-by-size heatmap, year-on-year movers, and auto-insights, plus a rich employer drill-down.
data_sources: WGEA Employer Gender Pay Gaps spreadsheet (wgea.gov.au)

sites.name: au-contracts
